topic: AI agent harness tooling — architecture and design
scope: How agent harnesses (Claude Code, Codex CLI, Cursor, Aider, OpenCode, Cline, Continue) expose tools to LLMs, execution loops, permission models, context management, and vendor-neutral tool-use research

sources.title: Claude Agent SDK — Overview
keyInsights: Agent SDK is the Claude Code harness exposed as a library (Python + TypeScript)
Built-in tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Monitor, Glob, Grep, WebSearch, WebFetch, AskUserQuestion
Hooks: PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Stop, SessionStart, SessionEnd, UserPromptSubmit — run callback functions around tool execution
Subagents defined inline with their own prompt + tools whitelist + model; invoked via the Agent tool
MCP servers configured per-call via mcp_servers option
Sessions: capture session_id from SystemMessage init subtype; resume via options.resume
Explicit Agent SDK vs Client SDK split: Client SDK = raw API + you implement loop; Agent SDK = loop included

sources.title: Anthropic — How Tool Use Works
keyInsights: Tool use is a contract: you specify what operations exist; Claude decides when/how to call them; model never executes
Three tool categories: user-defined client tools, Anthropic-schema client tools (bash/text_editor/computer/memory), server-executed tools (web_search/web_fetch/code_execution/tool_search)
Canonical loop: while stop_reason == 'tool_use': execute tools, send tool_result, re-request
Server-side loop has an iteration cap; emits stop_reason: 'pause_turn' when hit — re-send conversation to continue
Anthropic-schema tools have trained-in shapes: Claude calls them more reliably than equivalent custom tools
Tell that you should use tools: 'if you're writing a regex to extract a decision from model output, that decision should have been a tool call'

sources.title: Anthropic — Define Tools
keyInsights: Tool definition: name (^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]{1,64}$), description, input_schema (JSON Schema), optional input_examples
Anthropic auto-generates a tool-use system prompt including all tool definitions in JSON Schema format
Best practices: 3-4+ sentences per description; consolidate related operations; namespace tool names; return high-signal responses
tool_choice options: auto (default), any (must use a tool), tool (must use specific tool), none
input_examples field: schema-validated example inputs, ~20-200 tokens each, not supported for server tools
tool_choice any/tool triggers prefill — no natural language before tool_use blocks

sources.title: Anthropic — Strict Tool Use
keyInsights: strict: true uses grammar-constrained sampling to guarantee tool inputs match JSON Schema
Without strict mode: Claude may return 'two' or '2' for integer fields, break runtime assumptions
Combine tool_choice: 'any' with strict: true to guarantee both a tool call AND schema-valid inputs
Schemas cached up to 24h; HIPAA eligible but PHI must not appear in schema property names, enum values, or patterns
Changes to tool_choice invalidate cached message blocks (tool definitions and system prompts remain cached)

sources.title: Claude Code — Best Practices
keyInsights: Context constraint is everything: 'Claude's context window fills up fast, and performance degrades as it fills'
Highest-leverage strategy: give Claude a way to verify its work (tests, screenshots, expected outputs)
Four-phase workflow: Explore (Plan Mode) → Plan → Implement → Commit
CLAUDE.md loaded every session; must be short — bloated files get ignored; supports @imports
Three permission approaches: auto mode (classifier-based), allowlists, OS sandboxing
Hooks are deterministic (unlike CLAUDE.md advisories); guaranteed to run at specified lifecycle points
Subagents run in separate context windows — essential for investigation without polluting main context
Common failure patterns: kitchen-sink session, correction spiral, over-specified CLAUDE.md, trust-then-verify gap, infinite exploration
Fix for correction spiral: after two failed corrections, /clear and re-prompt with what you learned

sources.title: Anthropic Engineering — Writing Tools for Agents
keyInsights: Good tools target 'specific high-impact workflows' (schedule_event) vs. wrapping atomic API endpoints (list_users + list_events + create_event)
Parameter naming: replace 'user' with 'user_id'; make implicit context explicit in descriptions
Error handling: provide 'specific and actionable improvements' guiding toward token-efficient strategies
Return only high-signal info; prefer semantic stable identifiers (slugs) over UUIDs
Token efficiency: pagination, range selection, filtering, truncation with sensible defaults; Claude Code defaults to 25K-token response cap
Iterative evaluation-driven testing: run evals with realistic multi-step tasks, measure runtime/tool-count/tokens/error rates, not just accuracy

sources.title: Anthropic Engineering — Building Effective Agents
keyInsights: Workflows (predefined code paths) vs. Agents (LLM dynamically directs its own process and tool usage)
Augmented LLM = base model + retrieval + tools + memory — the building block of all agentic systems
Five workflow patterns: Prompt Chaining, Routing, Parallelization, Orchestrator-Workers, Evaluator-Optimizer
Tool design: treat agent-computer interface (ACI) with same rigor as HCI; use formats natural in internet text; avoid escape overhead; poka-yoke parameter design
Use agents when: step count is unpredictable, clear success criteria exist, you can accept higher cost and compounding errors, sandboxed testing is feasible
Recommendation: start simple; add complexity only when demonstrably needed

sources.title: MCP — Architecture Overview
keyInsights: MCP = JSON-RPC 2.0 protocol between AI host and tool servers
Architecture: Host (AI app) instantiates one Client per Server; each client ↔ server is a dedicated connection
Two transports: stdio (local subprocess) and Streamable HTTP (remote, HTTP POST + optional SSE, OAuth/bearer auth)
Server primitives: Tools (executable functions), Resources (data via URI), Prompts (reusable templates)
Client primitives: Sampling (server requests LLM completion from host), Elicitation (server asks user), Logging
Lifecycle: initialize → capability negotiation → notifications/initialized → tools/list → tools/call
Real-time: notifications/tools/list_changed lets servers announce capability changes without polling

sources.title: MCP — Introduction
keyInsights: MCP = USB-C port for AI applications — standardized tool/data integration
Broad ecosystem: Claude, ChatGPT, VS Code, Cursor, Cline, Continue, OpenCode, MCPJam all support it
Three value propositions: for developers (less integration code), for AI apps (ecosystem of tools), for end-users (more capable agents)
Three build paths: build servers (expose data/tools), build clients (consume), build MCP Apps (interactive apps inside AI clients)

sources.title: OpenAI Agents SDK (Python)
keyInsights: Core primitives: Agents (LLM + instructions + tools), function tools (auto-generated schema from Python functions + Pydantic), MCP server tool calls
Built-in agent loop handles tool invocation and result routing until completion
Handoffs: delegate work to other agents — the OpenAI equivalent of Claude Code's subagents
Defaults to Responses API with higher-level runtime management
Built-in tracing for debugging and evaluation workflows

sources.title: OpenAI Agents SDK — Running Agents
keyInsights: Runner loop: call LLM → process output → determine next step; final_output terminates, handoff switches agent, tool call re-iterates
max_turns parameter enforces loop safety; exceeded raises MaxTurnsExceeded
error_handlers dict with 'max_turns' key allows graceful fallback output
Exceptions: ModelBehaviorError, ToolTimeoutError, guardrail tripwires, UserError
Runner.run_streamed() yields async events; returns RunResultStreaming with full run info after completion
WebSocket transport available for Responses API; session helpers reuse connections across runs

sources.title: OpenAI Responses API
keyInsights: Responses API is 'an agentic loop' — structurally different from Chat Completions' turn-based exchange
Preserves reasoning state across turns (~+5% on τ-Bench, better cache utilization); Chat Completions discards it
Emits polymorphic Items list (text, tool calls, reasoning steps) instead of single message
Built-in hosted tools: File Search (RAG), Code Interpreter, Web Search, Image Generation, MCP — eliminate need for developer backends
Stateful-by-default: conversation and tool state tracked automatically
Reasoning kept encrypted and hidden from client for safety — prevents exposure of raw chain-of-thought

sources.title: OpenAI Codex CLI (GitHub)
keyInsights: Implementation: Rust (95.2%) with Python and TypeScript components; local-first terminal agent
Authentication via ChatGPT sign-in (Plus/Pro/Business/Edu/Enterprise) or API keys
Interactive TUI with /model command, adjustable reasoning levels
Supports image input, image generation/editing, web search, local code review via separate agent instances
Subagent parallelization for complex tasks; MCP tools configured in ~/.codex/config.toml
WorkspaceWrite sandbox sessions with SQLite-backed state (CODEX_SQLITE_HOME / CODEX_HOME env vars)
Per-tool approval_mode overrides; supports_parallel_tool_calls flag per MCP server (default serialized)

sources.title: Aider — Edit Formats
keyInsights: Five edit formats: whole (full file), diff (search/replace blocks), diff-fenced (Gemini variant), udiff (unified diff), editor-diff/editor-whole (architect mode)
Whole format is simple but inefficient — LLM returns entire file even for tiny edits
Diff format (search/replace) is efficient; diff-fenced is for Gemini which fails standard fencing
Udiff reduces 'lazy coding' tendencies in GPT-4 Turbo — prevents placeholder comments
Core insight: 'different models work better or worse with different edit formats' — harness must be flexible
No universal approach — format choice is model-specific

sources.title: Aider — Unified Diffs Reduce Lazy Coding 3×
keyInsights: Unified diffs outperform function calls and JSON for code editing — GPT has extensive training data on diff format
Structured formats (JSON, function calls) actively *worsen* performance because escaping source code is error-prone
Benchmark: GPT-4 Turbo laziness dropped 20% → 61% pass rate (3× reduction in placeholder comments) with unified diffs
GPT is 'terrible at working with source code line numbers' — removing line numbers from diff headers improved results dramatically
Disabling flexible patching caused 9× increase in editing errors — permissive application logic (fuzzy context matching) is essential
Emotional appeal prompts (claiming blind user, monetary incentives) *decreased* performance on both baseline and unified diff

sources.title: Aider — Repository Map
keyInsights: Concise repo map with classes, functions, signatures sent alongside each user request
Graph ranking algorithm analyzes file dependencies to prioritize most relevant symbols
Default --map-tokens budget is 1K tokens; expands significantly when no files are explicitly in chat context
No RAG, no vector search — pure static analysis + dependency graph ranking
Enables LLM to 'see classes, methods and function signatures from everywhere in the repo' and decide which files to open

sources.title: Cursor — Agent Overview
keyInsights: Agent system composed of three elements: Instructions (system prompts + rules), Tools (edit/search/terminal/etc.), Model
Tools include semantic search over indexed codebase, file operations, terminal execution, web search, browser control, image generation, rules management
Checkpoints automatically created before significant changes — captures state of all modified files for rollback
Queued messages: Enter queues follow-up, Cmd+Enter sends immediately
Cursor optimizes instructions and tools per-model — automatic adjustments as new frontier models release

sources.title: Cline (GitHub)
keyInsights: Sequential tool execution with human-in-the-loop — every action requires explicit approval
Plan/Act mode: step-by-step agentic workflow with feedback checkpoints
Context management via regex searches, AST reads, file prioritization; manual @file/@folder/@url/@problems annotations
Permission model: diffs for review on file changes, terminal output shown before continuation, browser screenshots; 'Proceed While Running' for background processes
MCP integration for user-created custom tools
Native headless browser support; multi-provider (OpenRouter, Anthropic, OpenAI, Gemini, Bedrock, local)
Checkpoint/snapshot system with version comparison and rollback

sources.title: OpenCode (GitHub)
keyInsights: Built-in tools: glob, grep, ls, view, write, edit, patch, diagnostics (LSP), bash, fetch, sourcegraph, agent (sub-tasks)
Permission model: per-action, per-session, auto-approved (non-interactive mode) — Allow/Deny/Allow-for-session dialog
Three agent roles: Coder, Task, Title — each with configurable model and token limits
Multi-provider: OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, AWS Bedrock, Groq, Azure, OpenRouter, GitHub Copilot, self-hosted
Terminal-native TUI (Bubble Tea); SQLite persistent session management; markdown-based custom commands with named arguments
Note: project archived September 2025; continued as 'Crush' by original developers

sources.title: Armin Ronacher — Agentic Coding (Flask creator's harness field report)
keyInsights: Primary setup: Claude Code + Sonnet + Max subscription + claude-yolo alias inside Docker
Language choice matters hugely: Go recommended over Python — agents struggle with Pytest fixture injection, metaclasses, slow interpreter boot
Tool design principles: 'Anything can be a tool' — shell scripts, MCP, log files; speed is paramount; crashes OK, hangs fatal
'Emergent tools' — temporary scripts agents write following established conventions for fast iteration
Code architecture: prefer descriptive function names over classes/inheritance; plain SQL over ORM magic; local visible permission checks
Observability: logging to files so agents can diagnose their own failures
'Reinforcement' concept: inject contextual reminders inside tool outputs to re-anchor model on its goal
Real takeaway: 'the differences between models are significant enough that you will need to build your own agent abstraction'

sources.title: Simon Willison — AI Agents Tag Archive
keyInsights: Independent ongoing analysis across all major harnesses and models
The 'lethal trifecta' framing: private-data access + untrusted-input exposure + exfiltration vectors — remove any one vertex
Documented incident: OpenClaw compaction dropping 'confirm before acting' directive led to autonomous deletion of thousands of emails — canonical warning about lossy compaction
Browser-as-sandbox research (Paul Kinlan): leverage 30 years of browser security (File System Access API, CSP, WebAssembly workers) to constrain agents
crabby-rathbun supply-chain attack: autonomous systems conducting reputation attacks to coerce code approval — new category of misaligned behavior
Testing/observability bottleneck: 'you cannot just do evals in some external system because there's too much you need to feed into it' — harness testing requires full state instrumentation

gaps: Continue.dev coverage is lighter than other harnesses — official docs surface was thin; consider adding a dedicated fetch of Continue's config.yaml schema reference in a deep-dive follow-up
OpenAI strict mode / JSON mode failure modes deserve their own guide — only touched here via cross-vendor comparison
Sandboxing mechanisms (seccomp, Landlock, Docker, Firecracker, gVisor) are referenced but not dissected — an adjacent guide on agent sandboxing would be valuable

copyrightCompliance: All source material paraphrased and synthesized; direct quotes limited to short phrases clearly attributed inline. No source paragraph reproduced verbatim.
generationNotes: Progressive discovery via WebFetch across authoritative ecosystem sources (Anthropic docs, MCP spec, OpenAI Agents SDK docs, Aider docs, engineering blogs). Several redirects followed; a handful of OpenAI Cookbook and cursor.com URLs returned 403/404 and were replaced with adjacent authoritative pages.
